nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing rocky rapids and boulder-strewn stretches of blackwater/clearwater rivers; young inhabit shallow rapid zones under flat rocks, adults occupy larger cavities among and beneath submerged boulders

decorPreferences: large smooth boulders and rocky caves replicating Xingu rapids
smooth flat rocks for aufwuchs grazing
ceramic or slate breeding caves with snug opening
driftwood for additional biofilm and territory definition
minimal fine substrate — sand or bare-bottom acceptable
strong directional flow (4–5× tank volume per hour)
high oxygenation via surface agitation or airstones

diet.notes: Herbivore-leaning omnivore; primary diet is algae (diatoms, filamentous genera such as Spirogyra) and biofilm grazed from rock surfaces. Supplement with high-quality sinking algae wafers, spirulina-based pellets, and blanched vegetables (zucchini, sweet potato, cucumber). Occasional protein supplementation — frozen bloodworm, brine shrimp, or high-protein gel food — supports growth and conditioning. Allow aufwuchs to colonise tank surfaces. Avoid overfeeding, which rapidly degrades water quality. Feed after lights-out as the species is primarily nocturnal.

goodWith: medium-to-large characids (e.g., silver dollars, larger tetras)
peaceful mid-water cichlids of similar size
avoidWith: other Gold Nugget Plecos (intense conspecific territorial aggression in males)
small fish under 5 cm (risk of being harassed or crushed against substrate)
aggressive cichlids that compete for cave territories
species requiring cool or soft-water conditions incompatible with warm Xingu parameters

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase specimen photo of Baryancistrus xanthellus showing characteristic yellow-spotted pattern.

summary: A striking armored catfish from the fast-flowing rocky rapids of the Rio Xingu and its tributaries in Brazil, the Gold Nugget Pleco is prized for its bold yellow polka-dot pattern on a dark body with yellow-edged fins. Adults reach 25–30 cm and become strongly territorial toward conspecifics. Intermediate care due to warm-water and high-flow requirements.
careNotes:
The Gold Nugget Pleco (Baryancistrus xanthellus) originates from the fast-flowing, highly oxygenated boulder rapids of the Rio Xingu and Rio Iriri in Pará state, Brazil — a habitat threatened by the Belo Monte hydroelectric dam. This background dictates almost all husbandry requirements: the species demands warm water (27–30°C), excellent oxygenation, strong directional flow (4–5× tank volume per hour), and pristine water quality with near-zero nitrate/nitrite. A minimum 380 L tank with a footprint of at least 150 cm is the established consensus for a single adult long-term; larger setups improve health and reduce disease.

L-number synonymy: L18, L85, and LDA060 are accepted as Baryancistrus xanthellus; L81 ("stardust" form with finer spots, from São Félix do Xingu) is provisionally treated as the same species but may represent a distinct taxon — it is often sold separately in the trade. L177 (large-spot gold nugget from the lower Xingu) is also assigned to B. xanthellus. All trade forms require identical husbandry. Merge candidate fw-fish-361 ("Baryancistrus spp.") represents the same trade group.

Males are strongly territorial toward conspecifics once sexually mature (around 3–4 years) and should not be kept together without very large setups and multiple cave territories. Juveniles are comparatively peaceful. Compatible with robust mid-water community fish that do not compete for bottom territory.

Biofilm and algae grazing are central to long-term health; allow rock surfaces to colonise with aufwuchs and provide sinking vegetable-content wafers, blanched vegetables, and occasional frozen protein. The species is sensitive to copper-based medications and cannot tolerate organic waste accumulation — weekly 25–30% water changes and robust mechanical/biological filtration are essential. Cost and sensitivity make this an intermediate-level species despite not requiring specialist marine or specialist breeding setups.

[Merge consolidation 2026-06-10]: Consolidated with retired entry fw-fish-361 (Gold Nugget Pleco / Baryancistrus spp.). B. xanthellus is the formally described species covering L18/L81/L85/L177 trade L-numbers in the Gold Nugget complex; "Baryancistrus spp." is the trade umbrella label.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is extremely rare and considered one of the most challenging feats in the pleco hobby. B. xanthellus is a cave spawner: males select and defend a snug cave (ceramic tube or slate cave with ~6–7 cm opening), court females, and guard the adhesive egg clutch until hatching (approximately 7–10 days). The male fans and guards the fry through yolk-sac absorption. Success requires a very large, mature aquarium (400 L+), pristine water, strong flow replicating the Xingu rapids, superior conditioning of both sexes over months on a varied diet, and appropriate caves. Fish must reach 3–4 years and 12+ cm before breeding condition is achieved. Fry grow slowly (approximately 1 cm per 2 months) and are sensitive to nitrate; grow-out water should be kept below 10 ppm nitrate. Only a handful of hobbyist successes have been documented worldwide.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Baryancistrus xanthellus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided temperature 27–32°C, pH 6.4–7.6, hardness 2–14°H, minimum tank base 120×45 cm, diet (aufwuchs/algae with protein supplement), conspecific aggression profile, L-number assignments (L018, L085, L177, LDA060), native range (Volta Grande do Xingu and Rio Iriri), and breeding difficulty notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Baryancistrus xanthellus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (29.7 cm TL, 510 g), taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes, Hypostominae), native range (Volta Grande do Xingu above Belo Monte falls and Rio Iriri), habitat (shallow rapid stretches under rocks for young; large boulder cavities for adults), diet (algae, bryozoans, chironomid larvae). IUCN Near Threatened status noted.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Gold Nugget Plecos: Treasure from the Xingu
additional.notes: Confirmed warm-water requirement (~86°F/30°C), high flow and oxygenation demands, Rio Xingu native range (Belo Monte to above São Félix do Xingu including Iriri), diet supplementation (zucchini, sweet potato, green-based foods), adult size ~23 cm reported in article context, L-number equivalences L018/L081/L085/L177/LDA060.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Baryancistrus sp. cf. xanthellus (L081)
additional.notes: Cross-reference for L081 variant. Confirmed temperature 27–32°C, pH 6.4–7.6, hardness 54–268 ppm, adult size 150–180 mm SL, tank base 120×45 cm minimum. L081 ("stardust") originates from São Félix do Xingu, upstream of confirmed B. xanthellus localities; provisionally same species but taxonomic status unresolved. Captive breeding noted as extremely rare.

additional.name: Aquariadise — Gold Nugget Pleco Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary trade source. Confirmed temperature 77–84°F (25–29°C), pH 6.5–7.5, gH 2–15, tank minimum 120 gallons (454 L) for long-term; used 380 L as conservative consensus. Lifespan ~10 years cited; 8–15 range adopted to reflect expert-kept longevity reports. Copper sensitivity confirmed. Nocturnal feeding schedule. Growth rate ~1 cm/2 months.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave spawner; male guards adhesive egg clutch and fry. Captive breeding documented only a handful of times globally. Requires mature 400 L+ setup, strong flow, pristine water, 3–4 years to sexual maturity, and prolonged conditioning. Fry grow ~1 cm per 2 months.
